title: Tick vs Backtick
subtitle: Two ways to land on the same mark — line, or exact spot.
summary: {key:'} (apostrophe) is linewise: jumps to the line of the mark, lands on first non-blank. {key:`} (backtick) is charwise: jumps to the exact stored line and column.

blocks.text:
Every mark has *two* ways to jump to it:

  • {key:'}{m} — jump to the **line** of mark m, cursor on first non-blank. Linewise.
  • {key:`}{m} — jump to the **exact position** of mark m. Charwise.
Mnemonic: tick (`'`) = top of column on the line. Backtick (`` ` ``) = back to the precise byte.

blocks.title: Which to Use Depends on the Operator
blocks.text:
When *jumping*, the difference is small: tick lands on first non-blank, backtick lands at the original column. Either is fine for navigation.

When used as a *motion* with an operator, the difference is huge:

  • {key:d}{key:'}{key:a} deletes from the current line through the line of mark `a`, **linewise**. Both lines and everything between them go in their entirety.
  • {key:d}{key:`}{key:a} deletes from the cursor to the exact position of mark `a`, **charwise**. Partial lines.

For copying chunks of code: tick. For surgical deletes: backtick. For navigation: whichever your finger reaches.

Backtick is awkward to type on most keyboards. Many users remap with `nnoremap ' `` ` `` and `nnoremap `` ` `` '` to swap them — backtick (the more useful one) becomes the easier key. Try it for a week.
